This is the brief on the biological engine of living things, known as life processes. You know, when you look at a sleeping dog or just a stationary plant, it's kind of wild to think about. If we can't see it moving, what's actually keeping it alive? Well, first, being alive really isn't about what we can see moving.

The true proof of life happens totally invisibly, right down at the microscopic molecular level. Even while resting, organisms are doing some seriously complex internal work. Think of it exactly like your smartphone running background app updates while the screen is completely black. So what are all these moving molecules actually doing?

They're doing maintenance. Second, these continuous 24-7 maintenance jobs are called life processes. Every single living organism has to perform this constant internal upkeep to stay alive, remain functional, and prevent physical wear and tear. But what actually powers this relentless biological upkeep?

You can't run a machine without fuel, which brings us to our last point. Finally, this fuel comes from a two-step system. Nutrition and respiration. Nutrition is simply the process of bringing in food from the outside to get raw materials.

Then respiration steps in, using oxygen to chemically break down that food, releasing energy the cells can actually use. These two processes literally fund everything else an organism does, from growth to tissue repair. Ultimately, being alive isn't about what you are visibly doing on the outside. It's about the microscopic, relentless machinery of nutrition and respiration, keeping your biological engine humming on the inside.
